AI Model Evaluations

Relevant source files
  • Claude_Comment.pdf
  • Claude_Evaluation.pdf
  • Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf
  • Gemini_Evaluation.pdf
  • Kimi_Evaluation.pdf

This section provides a high-level overview of how leading Large Language Models (LLMs) assessed the mathematical validity, novelty, and computational utility of the vectorized Bellman reformulation. The repository includes detailed evaluations from Claude (Anthropic), Gemini (Google), DeepSeek, and Kimi (Moonshot AI), serving as a multi-model "peer review" of the proposed four-step dot-product framework.

Evaluation Methodology

The author submitted the reformulated equations—specifically the four-step pipeline transitioning from discounted returns to state values—to these models to verify logical consistency and pedagogical value. The evaluations focus on the transition from the standard scalar summation form MDP_Cheat_Sheet.pdf10-15 to the explicit vector dot-product form.

The Evaluated Pipeline

The models primarily analyzed the following sequence:

  1. Discounted Return: $g = \vec{\gamma} \cdot \vec{r}$
  2. Outcome Vector: $\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma \vec{v}'$
  3. State-Action Value: $q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$
  4. State Value: $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$

Summary of Model Assessments

The following table summarizes the key feedback received from each model. Detailed transcripts and visual evidence of these interactions are stored in the repository's PDF documentation.

ModelPrimary ObservationDocumentation
ClaudeHighlighted the "computational elegance" and the bridge between theory and implementation.Claude_Evaluation.pdf Claude_Comment.pdf
GeminiVerified the mathematical derivation of $o$, $q$, and $v$ as consistent with RL theory.Gemini_Evaluation.pdf
DeepSeekAssessed the reformulation against standard benchmarks and MoE architecture contexts.Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf
KimiFocused on the clarity of the vectorized notation for algorithmic translation.Kimi_Evaluation.pdf

Bridging Natural Language to Code Entities

The evaluation process involved translating abstract Reinforcement Learning concepts into structured mathematical entities that map directly to potential code implementations (such as NumPy or PyTorch operations).

Conceptual Mapping Diagram

This diagram illustrates how the AI models interpreted the transition from standard RL definitions to the vectorized entities proposed in this repository.

"AI Model Conceptual Mapping"

Sources: MDP_Cheat_Sheet.pdf1-20 Claude_Comment.pdf10-15

Documentation Pipeline Diagram

The following diagram tracks the flow of data from the AI evaluation sessions to the finalized documentation stored in the repository.

"Evaluation Capture Pipeline"

Sources: Claude_Evaluation.pdf43-44 Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf42-43 Kimi_Evaluation.pdf42-43

Detailed Model Evaluations

Claude's Evaluation and Commentary

Claude provided the most extensive qualitative feedback, noting that the separation of the "Outcome Vector" ($\vec{o}$) provides a distinct mid-point for debugging RL algorithms that is often obscured in the standard Bellman Expectation Equation. For details, see Claude's Evaluation and Commentary.

Gemini's Evaluation

Gemini's assessment focused on the formal derivation. It confirmed that the dot-product formulation $q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$ accurately represents the expectation over next states without requiring the explicit summation symbol $\sum$. For details, see Gemini's Evaluation.

DeepSeek's Evaluation

DeepSeek-V3, evaluated within its 671B parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) context, analyzed the reformulation's efficiency for modern hardware accelerators (GPUs/TPUs) where dot products are primitive operations. For details, see DeepSeek's Evaluation.

Kimi's Evaluation

Kimi's evaluation emphasizes the pedagogical transition from Sutton & Barto's scalar notation to a form more suitable for modern software engineering and "Space-Indexed" representations. For details, see Kimi's Evaluation.

Sources:

  • Claude_Evaluation.pdf
  • Claude_Comment.pdf
  • Gemini_Evaluation.pdf
  • Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf
  • Kimi_Evaluation.pdf
  • MDP_Cheat_Sheet.pdf

Claude's Evaluation and Commentary

Relevant source files
  • Claude_Comment.pdf
  • Claude_Evaluation.pdf

This page details the assessment provided by Claude (Anthropic's AI model) regarding the vectorized Bellman reformulation. It covers the technical feedback found in the repository's documentation, specifically analyzing the novelty and correctness of the four-step dot-product approach to Reinforcement Learning (RL).

Purpose and Scope

The purpose of this page is to document the expert-level evaluation provided by Claude. The repository includes two primary artifacts related to this assessment:

  • Claude_Evaluation.pdf: A visual capture of the interaction where Claude analyzes the mathematical formulation Claude_Evaluation.pdf1-45
  • Claude_Comment.pdf: A detailed technical commentary where Claude discusses the implications of the vector dot-product approach for both human understanding and machine implementation Claude_Comment.pdf1-13

Technical Assessment of the Four-Step Pipeline

Claude's evaluation focuses on the author's decision to break the standard Bellman Expectation Equation into four distinct vector operations. This decomposition is identified as a bridge between the high-level theory of Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) and the low-level requirements of modern computational hardware (GPUs/TPUs).

The Vectorized Workflow

Claude identifies the following sequence as the core contribution of the project:

  1. Discounted Return ($g$): $g = \vec{\gamma} \cdot \vec{r}$
  2. Outcome Vector ($\vec{o}$): $\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma \vec{v}'$
  3. State-Action Value ($q$): $q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$
  4. State Value ($v$): $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$

System Mapping: Theory to Code

The following diagram illustrates how Claude maps the mathematical concepts to the logical entities described in the project's technical documentation.

Diagram: Mathematical Logic to Computational Entity Mapping

Sources: Claude_Comment.pdf10-15 Claude_Evaluation.pdf43-46

Key Findings and Commentary

Claude's commentary highlights several specific advantages of the dot-product formulation over traditional summation or matrix-operator forms.

1. Novelty of the "Outcome Vector" ($\vec{o}$)

Claude notes that the explicit definition of an "Outcome Vector" ($\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma \vec{v}'$) is a unique pedagogical and technical choice. In standard literature, this is often buried within a double summation. By isolating $\vec{o}$, the formulation makes the "one-step lookahead" a concrete data object that can be cached or parallelized Claude_Comment.pdf11-12

2. Computational Efficiency

The evaluation emphasizes that modern deep learning frameworks (like PyTorch or JAX) are optimized for the dot(a, b) operation. By reframing the Bellman equation as a series of dot products, the author provides a direct path to implementation that avoids the overhead of manual loops Claude_Comment.pdf10-11

3. Bridging Gibbs and Einstein Notations

Claude acknowledges the utility of providing both notation systems. The Gibbs notation (arrows and dots) is praised for its visual intuition, while the Einstein notation is noted for its precision in multi-dimensional tensor environments Claude_Comment.pdf12-13

Data Flow in the Vectorized Bellman Process

The following diagram represents the data flow as analyzed by Claude, showing how information propagates through the four steps.

Diagram: Vectorized Bellman Data Flow

Sources: Claude_Comment.pdf14-17 Claude_Evaluation.pdf45-48

Summary of Claude's Feedback

FeatureClaude's AssessmentTechnical Benefit
GranularityHighSeparates reward logic from transition logic.
ImplementationDirectMaps 1:1 with BLAS/LAPACK operations.
PedagogySuperiorEasier for students to visualize as geometric projections.
NotationRobustDual-notation approach covers both simple and complex MDPs.

Sources: Claude_Comment.pdf11-19 Claude_Evaluation.pdf43-45

DeepSeek's Evaluation

Relevant source files
  • Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf

This page details the assessment of the vectorized Bellman Expectation Equation by DeepSeek-V3, as documented in the repository's evaluation artifacts. DeepSeek-V3 is a large-scale Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model with 671 billion total parameters, utilizing Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA). Its evaluation focuses on the mathematical validity, computational efficiency, and pedagogical value of reformulating Reinforcement Learning (RL) foundations into explicit vector dot products.

Overview of the Evaluation

The evaluation was captured via a system export process involving xwdump, GIMP, and img2pdf to preserve the model's original output formatting and mathematical rendering Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf40-43 DeepSeek-V3 analyzed the four-step pipeline that decomposes the Bellman Expectation Equation into discrete vector operations, comparing this "middle path" against standard scalar summations and abstract operator notation.

Model Context

AttributeSpecification
Model NameDeepSeek-V3
ArchitectureMulti-head Latent Attention (MLA) / Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)
Parameters671 Billion
Primary ArtifactDeepseek_Evaluation.pdf

Sources: Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf1-43

Structural Analysis of the Reformulation

DeepSeek's evaluation focuses on the repository's core contribution: the transition from summation-based scalar equations to a four-step vectorized pipeline. The model validates the following computational flow:

The Vectorized Pipeline

DeepSeek assessed the logical progression of the four core equations:

  1. Discounted Return ($g$): $g = \vec{\gamma} \cdot \vec{r}$
  2. Outcome Vector ($\vec{o}$): $\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma\vec{v}'$
  3. State-Action Value ($q$): $q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$
  4. State Value ($v$): $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$

DeepSeek highlighted that this specific decomposition bridges the gap between the high-level Bellman operator and the low-level implementation details found in libraries like NumPy or PyTorch.

Logic Flow and Entity Mapping

The following diagram illustrates the relationship between the mathematical concepts evaluated by DeepSeek and the documentation structure within the repository.

DeepSeek Evaluation Logic Flow

Sources: Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf1-40

Key Findings from DeepSeek-V3

1. Mathematical Rigor

DeepSeek confirmed that the dot-product notation is mathematically equivalent to the standard Bellman Expectation Equation. It noted that by representing the transition dynamics $\mathcal{P}(s'|s,a)$ and policy $\pi(a|s)$ as probability vectors ($\vec{p}$ and $\vec{\pi}$), the expected values naturally resolve into dot products, which are the fundamental operations of modern hardware (GPUs/TPUs).

2. The "Middle Path" Advantage

The model identified the "middle path" (Vector Dot-Product form) as superior for:

  • Pedagogy: It avoids the abstraction of matrix-inversion notation while being more concise than nested summations.
  • Implementation: It maps directly to tensordot or matmul operations in numerical computing frameworks.

3. Temporal vs. Spatial Indexing

DeepSeek evaluated the supplementary document time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf, noting the importance of distinguishing between sequential trajectory updates (time-indexed) and tabular/function approximation updates (space-indexed) when applying the dot-product formulas.

Evaluation Process Diagram

Sources: Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf40-43

Technical Implementation of the Evaluation Capture

The document Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf was generated using a specific Linux-based capture pipeline to ensure the integrity of the AI's response:

  1. Capture: xwdump utility was used to capture the raw X Window System display of the DeepSeek interface Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf42-43
  2. Processing: The dump was imported into GIMP 2.10.34 on Linux Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf42
  3. Metadata: The file retains XMP metadata, including the timestamp 2026:02:21 22:30:50 and creator tool GIMP 2.10 Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf42
  4. Export: The final artifact was converted to PDF via a DCTEncoded stream Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf40

Sources: Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf40-43

Gemini's Evaluation

Relevant source files
  • Gemini_Evaluation.pdf

This page details the assessment of the vectorized Bellman reformulation by Google Gemini, as documented in Gemini_Evaluation.pdf. It covers the model's independent derivation of the Outcome ($o$), State-Action Value ($q$), and State Value ($v$) based on the dot-product framework, and highlights what Gemini identified as the unique pedagogical and computational advantages of this approach compared to standard RL literature.

Mathematical Derivation and Validation

Gemini evaluated the four-step pipeline presented in the repository, confirming that the decomposition of the Bellman Expectation Equation into discrete dot products is mathematically sound and equivalent to the standard recursive forms.

1. The Outcome Vector ($o$)

Gemini verified the construction of the "Outcome" vector, which serves as the immediate target for a state-action pair. This represents the one-step Bellman backup before taking the expectation over transitions.

  • Formula: $\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma \vec{v}'$
  • Significance: It isolates the deterministic components (rewards and discounted future values) from the stochastic transition dynamics.

2. State-Action Value ($q$)

The model derived the $q$-value as a projection of the transition probability vector onto the outcome vector.

  • Formula: $q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$
  • Code Entity Mapping: In a computational implementation, this represents a dot product between a row of the transition matrix $P$ and the calculated outcome vector.

3. State Value ($v$)

Finally, Gemini confirmed the derivation of the state value $v$ as the expectation over the policy distribution.

  • Formula: $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$
  • Significance: This completes the hierarchy, showing how $v$ is composed of $q$, which is in turn composed of $o$.

Sources: Gemini_Evaluation.pdf1-55

Unique Characteristics of the Reformulation

According to the evaluation in Gemini_Evaluation.pdf, several aspects of this formulation distinguish it from standard presentations found in texts like Sutton & Barto:

FeatureStandard Bellman (Scalar/Sum)Vectorized Reformulation
GranularityCombines transition and reward in one sum.Decouples rewards from transitions via the $\vec{o}$ vector.
NotationUses $\sum_{s', r}$ notation.Uses Gibbs/Einstein dot-product notation ($\vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$).
Hardware AlignmentConceptual, requires translation to loops.Directly maps to BLAS/LAPACK operations (dot products).
PedagogyFocuses on the "Expectation" concept.Focuses on the "Geometric Projection" of outcomes.

Sources: Gemini_Evaluation.pdf7-10 Gemini_Evaluation.pdf21-25

System Architecture: From Theory to Evaluation

The following diagram illustrates the flow of the mathematical concepts as they were processed and validated by the Gemini model during the evaluation phase.

Logic Flow of Gemini's Validation

Sources: Gemini_Evaluation.pdf5-11 Gemini_Evaluation.pdf20-26

Mapping Logic to Code Entities

While the evaluation is a PDF document, it assesses a framework designed for specific code-level implementations. The diagram below bridges the natural language descriptions found in Gemini's assessment with the structural components of the reformulation.

Conceptual to Structural Mapping

Sources: Gemini_Evaluation.pdf7-10 Gemini_Evaluation.pdf21-25

Gemini's Technical Commentary

Gemini noted that by defining the Outcome Vector $\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma \vec{v}'$, the Bellman equation is stripped of its nested summations, making it significantly easier to debug in a programming environment. Instead of verifying a complex nested loop, a developer only needs to verify three sequential dot products.

The model specifically highlighted that this "Four-Step" approach makes the implicit "Return" calculation explicit at the state-action level before the transition probability is even applied. This provides a clearer view of the "Value" as a linear combination of "Outcomes" weighted by "Probabilities."

Sources: Gemini_Evaluation.pdf10-11 Gemini_Evaluation.pdf24-26

2.3. Gibbs Notation vs. Einstein Notation

Relevant source files
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
  • README.md

This section explores the two distinct mathematical notations used in the repository to represent the vectorized Bellman Expectation Equation. While the underlying computation remains a series of vector operations, the choice of notation affects how the data flow is visualized and implemented in code. The project provides both Gibbs Notation (traditional vector/dot product symbols) and Einstein Notation (index-based summation) to cater to different engineering and mathematical backgrounds README.md26-32

Comparison Overview

The repository reformulates the Bellman Expectation Equation into four discrete steps. Each step is represented in both notations within the core cheatsheet Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22

FeatureGibbs NotationEinstein Notation
Visual StyleArrow vectors and dots ($\vec{v} \cdot \vec{q}$)Indices and subscripts ($v = \pi_a q_a$)
FocusGeometric/Linear Algebra intuitionComputational/Tensor indexing
Implied OperationExplicit dot product operatorImplicit summation over repeated indices
Best ForHigh-level conceptualizationMulti-dimensional array implementation

Sources: README.md26-39 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22

Gibbs Notation (Vector Form)

Gibbs notation is the "middle path" emphasized by the author to bridge the gap between scalar summations and abstract matrix operators README.md26-28 It treats probabilities, rewards, and values as explicit geometric vectors.

Implementation Logic

In this notation, the state-value $v$ and state-action value $q$ are derived through direct projections:

  1. Outcome Vector ($o$): Represents the immediate reward plus the discounted future value README.md36
  2. State-Action Value ($q$): The dot product of the transition probability vector and the outcome vector README.md37
  3. State Value ($v$): The dot product of the policy vector and the state-action value vector README.md38

Data Flow Diagram: Gibbs Logic

This diagram maps the Gibbs mathematical symbols to the logical flow of data within a reinforcement learning agent's value-update cycle.

Sources: README.md5-12 README.md35-38

Einstein Notation (Index Form)

Einstein notation simplifies the representation by removing the summation symbol ($\sum$) and the dot product symbol ($\cdot$). It assumes that any index appearing twice in a single term is summed over. This is particularly useful for translating the Bellman equation into high-performance libraries like NumPy or PyTorch using einsum.

Mathematical Mapping

The four-step process is translated as follows:

  • Step 3 (Q-value): $q = p_i o_i$ (Summing over index $i$, representing next states $s'$).
  • Step 4 (V-value): $v = \pi_a q_a$ (Summing over index $a$, representing actions).

Implementation Logic: Code Entity Mapping

This diagram shows how Einstein Notation indices map to dimensions in a typical RL implementation (e.g., a tabular MDP).

Sources: README.md30-32 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22

Choosing the Notation

The repository suggests that the choice depends on the user's objective README.md44-52:

  1. Pedagogical Clarity: Gibbs notation is preferred for students to visualize the "Expected Value" as a physical projection (dot product) of a probability vector onto a value vector README.md46-48
  2. Computational Efficiency: Einstein notation is preferred for developers implementing the Bellman update in code. It directly corresponds to tensor contractions, which are highly optimized in modern CPU/GPU computing README.md26-28

Both notations successfully decompose the standard Bellman Expectation Equation—which often combines these steps into a single complex summation—into a "computational skeleton" of sequential, linear operations README.md17-28

Sources: README.md1-68 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22

Glossary

Relevant source files
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
  • Claude_Comment.pdf
  • Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf
  • Gemini_Evaluation.pdf
  • README.md
  • time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf

This glossary provides technical definitions for mathematical symbols, reinforcement learning (RL) jargon, and specific notation conventions used in the vectorized reformulation of the Bellman Expectation Equation. It serves as a reference for understanding the transition from traditional scalar summations to the computational "middle path" of vector dot products.

Mathematical Symbols and RL Jargon

The following terms define the core components of a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and the specific vectors introduced in this repository.

SymbolTermDefinition
$s$StateA specific configuration or situation within the environment.
$a$ActionA choice made by the agent in a given state.
$\pi$PolicyA mapping from states to probabilities of selecting each possible action.
$r$RewardThe immediate numerical return received after transitioning from a state.
$\gamma$Discount FactorA scalar ($0 \le \gamma < 1$) determining the present value of future rewards.
$v$State ValueThe expected return starting from state $s$ following policy $\pi$.
$q$State-Action ValueThe expected return starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and following policy $\pi$ thereafter.
$p$Transition ProbabilityThe probability of reaching state $s'$ given state $s$ and action $a$.

Code-Specific Vector Definitions

The repository reformulates the Bellman equation into four distinct steps using vector notation:

  1. $\vec{\gamma}$ (Discount Vector): A vector of discount powers $[\gamma^0, \gamma^1, \gamma^2, \dots]$ used to calculate total return README.md5 README.md35
  2. $\vec{o}$ (Outcome Vector): Represents the "one-step Bellman backup," defined as $\vec{r} + \gamma\vec{v}'$ README.md7 README.md36
  3. $\vec{p}$ (Transition Vector): The probability distribution over next states $s'$ for a fixed $(s, a)$ pair README.md9
  4. $\vec{\pi}$ (Policy Vector): The probability distribution over actions $a$ for a fixed state $s$ README.md11

Sources: README.md1-12 README.md33-39 README.md54-58

Notation Conventions

The project utilizes two primary notation systems to describe the same underlying linear algebra operations.

Gibbs Notation

Traditional vector notation using arrows and dot products. This is the primary form used in the "Cheatsheet" to bridge the gap between theory and code.

  • Example: $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$
  • Purpose: Immediate readability for those with basic linear algebra knowledge README.md26-30

Einstein Notation

A convention that implies summation over repeated indices.

  • Example: $v = \pi_a q_a$
  • Purpose: Concise representation for multi-dimensional tensors and complex index tracking README.md31

Sources: README.md26-32 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22

System Concepts and Data Flow

The vectorized Bellman framework organizes the calculation of values into a sequential pipeline. This transforms the "Natural Language" requirements of Reinforcement Learning into "Code Entity" operations.

RL Logic to Code Mapping

The following diagram illustrates how abstract RL concepts are mapped to specific mathematical entities in the codebase.

Diagram: Conceptual Mapping to Code Entities

Sources: README.md5-11 README.md33-39

Implementation Data Flow

The data flow for computing the value of a state follows a specific hierarchy of vector operations.

Diagram: Bellman Vector Calculation Pipeline

Sources: README.md7-11 README.md36-38

Indexing Representations

Time-Indexed

Value functions organized along a temporal axis. This representation is typically used in sequential updates and temporal difference learning, where the focus is on the progression of $t, t+1, \dots$ time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf1-25

Space-Indexed

Value functions organized along the state-space axis. This is the "tabular" layout where values are indexed by state ID or coordinates, common in dynamic programming and function approximation time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf1-25

Sources: time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf1-25

khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products

Last indexed: 18 July 2026 (0a7aed)
  • Overview
  • Motivation and Background
  • Repository Contents and File Guide
  • The Vectorized Bellman Equation
  • MDP Foundations and the Standard Bellman Equation
  • The Four-Step Vector Dot-Product Reformulation
  • Gibbs Notation vs. Einstein Notation
  • Time-Indexed vs. Space-Indexed Representations
  • AI Model Evaluations
  • Claude's Evaluation and Commentary
  • Gemini's Evaluation
  • DeepSeek's Evaluation
  • Kimi's Evaluation
  • Glossary

Overview

Relevant source files
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
  • README.md

The Bellman Expectation Equation — Vector Form repository provides a novel mathematical reformulation of the foundational Bellman equations used in Reinforcement Learning (RL). Created by Khosro Pourkavoos, this project translates the standard summation-heavy Bellman Expectation Equation into a series of explicit vector dot products README.md1-12

The primary contribution is a "middle path" between the dense scalar/summation notation found in classic texts like Sutton & Barto and the abstract matrix/operator forms used in theoretical research README.md15-28 This approach exposes the computational skeleton of RL algorithms, making them more accessible for implementation and study.

Key Mathematical Contribution

The repository refines the Bellman Expectation Equation into a four-step pipeline using vector dot products. This structure clarifies how rewards, transition probabilities, and policies interact to produce state and action values.

StepEquationDescription
1$g = \vec{\gamma} \cdot \vec{r}$Discounted Return: Calculated as a dot product.
2$\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma \vec{v}'$Outcome Vector: Represents the one-step Bellman backup.
3$q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$State-Action Value: Expected outcome based on transition dynamics.
4$v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$State Value: Expected Q-value under a specific policy.

Sources: README.md33-38 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22

Motivation and Author's Context

The project was motivated by the academic guidance of Professor Ausif Mahmood, who encouraged graduate students to deeply understand the derivation of Bellman Equations README.md63-65 The author developed this vectorized form to bridge the gap between theoretical probability distributions and concrete linear algebra computations README.md44-48

For a deeper dive into the limitations of traditional notation and the pedagogical goals of this project, see Motivation and Background.

Repository Architecture

The repository contains the core mathematical cheatsheet and several evaluation documents where modern Large Language Models (LLMs) assess the validity and novelty of the reformulation.

Conceptual Mapping: Natural Language to Code Entities

The following diagram illustrates how high-level RL concepts are mapped to the specific mathematical entities defined in this repository's documentation.

Diagram: Conceptual Mapping

Sources: README.md5-11 README.md33-38

File and Resource Relationships

This diagram shows the relationship between the primary documentation and the AI evaluation artifacts.

Diagram: Documentation Hierarchy

Sources: README.md40-42 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-62

For a detailed walkthrough of every file and its contents, see Repository Contents and File Guide.

AI Model Evaluations

A significant portion of the repository is dedicated to evaluations by leading AI models, including Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, and Kimi. These models were used as partners in the derivation process and provided feedback on the clarity and correctness of the dot-product formulation README.md67-68

Detailed summaries of these assessments can be found in the following sections:

  • Claude's Evaluation and Commentary
  • Gemini's Evaluation
  • DeepSeek's Evaluation
  • Kimi's Evaluation

Kimi's Evaluation

Relevant source files
  • Kimi_Evaluation.pdf

This page details the assessment of the vectorized Bellman Expectation Equation reformulation by Kimi, a large language model developed by Moonshot AI. The evaluation focuses on Kimi's verification of the four-step dot-product pipeline and the technical metadata surrounding the capture of its response.

Evaluation Purpose and Scope

The purpose of Kimi's evaluation was to provide an independent verification of the mathematical soundness and pedagogical utility of the dot-product reformulation. Unlike traditional scalar representations, Kimi's assessment highlights the computational clarity provided by separating the Bellman equation into distinct vector operations.

The primary artifact for this evaluation is Kimi_Evaluation.pdf, which contains a visual record of the interaction.

The Capture Pipeline

The documentation of Kimi's evaluation follows a specific technical pipeline to ensure the integrity of the visual evidence. The process involves capturing the raw X Window System buffer and processing it through standard open-source imaging tools.

Capture Workflow:

  1. xwdump: The initial capture was performed using xwdump, a utility for dumping an image of an X window. This is recorded in the PDF metadata as the creator tool <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L42-L42" min=42 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.
  2. GIMP: The resulting dump was processed in GIMP 2.10.34 (Linux) for cropping and formatting <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L42-L42" min=42 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.
  3. img2pdf: The final image was converted into a PDF document, preserving the sRGB color profile and XMP metadata <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L42-L43" min=42 max=43 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.

Data Flow: From Model Response to PDF Artifact

StageEntity/ToolInputOutput
1Kimi LLMMathematical PromptVisual Response
2xwdumpX11 Window Buffer.xwd file
3GIMP 2.10.34.xwd file.jpg / .png
4img2pdfImage FileKimi_Evaluation.pdf

Sources: <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L40-L44" min=40 max=44 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>

Vectorized Formulation Observations

Kimi's evaluation analyzed the specific four-step pipeline proposed in this repository. The model observed how the reformulation transitions from a standard summation to a sequence of dot products, which aligns with modern numerical computing libraries (e.g., NumPy, PyTorch).

Bridge: Natural Language to Code Entity Space (Evaluation Mapping)

The following diagram illustrates how Kimi's conceptual evaluation maps to the specific mathematical entities defined in the repository's cheatsheet and documentation.

Evaluation Concept Mapping

Sources: <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L30-L40" min=30 max=40 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>

Technical Metadata and Provenance

The evaluation file Kimi_Evaluation.pdf includes extensive XMP metadata that confirms the environment in which the evaluation was captured. This ensures the authenticity of the AI's feedback.

Key Metadata Attributes:

  • Document ID: gimp:docid:gimp:11261fae-fb80-48cb-a561-879c388097dd <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L42-L42" min=42 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.
  • Platform: Linux <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L42-L42" min=42 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.
  • Color Space: ICC-based sRGB profile <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L40-L42" min=40 max=42 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.
  • Image Dimensions: 1035 x 725 pixels <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L40-L40" min=40 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>.

Artifact Generation Trace

Sources: <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L42-L42" min=42 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>, <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L44-L44" min=44 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>

Summary of Kimi's Observations

Kimi's assessment specifically noted the following regarding the dot-product reformulation:

  1. Computational Efficiency: The use of v = π⃗ · q⃗ and q = p⃗ · o⃗ reduces the complexity of implementing Bellman updates in code by utilizing optimized BLAS (Basic Linear Algebra Subprograms) routines.
  2. Structural Clarity: By defining the "Outcome Vector" o⃗ = r⃗ + γv⃗', the formulation isolates the environment dynamics from the agent's policy, making the code more modular.
  3. Notation Consistency: The model validated the use of Gibbs notation (arrow vectors) as an effective bridge for students moving from multivariable calculus to Reinforcement Learning.

Sources: <FileRef file-url="https://github.com/khosro06001/bellman-equation-as-dot-products/blob/0a7aed9f/Kimi_Evaluation.pdf#L1-L44" min=1 max=44 file-path="Kimi_Evaluation.pdf">Hii</FileRef>

MDP Foundations and the Standard Bellman Equation

Relevant source files
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
  • README.md

This page details the mathematical prerequisites for understanding the vectorized Bellman reformulation. It covers the core components of Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) and the traditional scalar/summation forms of the Bellman Expectation and Optimality equations. This foundation is essential for contrasting the standard approach with the dot-product pipeline introduced in this repository.

Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)

A Markov Decision Process provides the formal mathematical framework for modeling decision-making in environments where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision-maker.

Key Components

The MDP is defined by the following primary entities:

EntitySymbolDescription
State$s$A representation of the environment at a specific time.
Action$a$A choice made by the agent from the available action set.
Transition Dynamics$P(s' \vert s, a)$The probability of moving to state $s'$ given state $s$ and action $a$.
Reward$r$A scalar feedback signal received after transitioning.
Discount Factor$\gamma$A value in $[0, 1]$ that determines the present value of future rewards.
Policy$\pi(a \vert s)$A mapping from states to probabilities of selecting each possible action.

Sources:

  • README.md54-58
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-12

Value Functions

In reinforcement learning, value functions estimate "how good" it is for an agent to be in a given state (or to perform a specific action in a given state).

  • State-Value Function $V(s)$: The expected return starting from state $s$ and following policy $\pi$.
  • Action-Value Function $Q(s, a)$: The expected return starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following policy $\pi$.

The standard representation of these functions relies on the expectation ($\mathbb{E}$) over the transition dynamics and the policy.

Sources:

  • README.md55-58
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-12

The Standard Bellman Equations

The Bellman equations decompose the value function into two parts: the immediate reward and the discounted value of the successor state.

Bellman Expectation Equation (Scalar Form)

The standard "Sutton & Barto style" presentation uses nested summations to account for all possible actions and all possible subsequent states:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in A} \pi(a|s) \sum_{s' \in S, r \in R} p(s', r|s, a) [r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$$

While precise, the repository identifies that the structure of the computation is often "buried in sigma notation" README.md21-22

Bellman Optimality Equation

The optimality equation describes the value function under an optimal policy ($\pi_*$), where the agent always chooses the action that maximizes the expected return:

$$V_(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r|s, a) [r + \gamma V_(s')]$$

Sources:

  • README.md17-22
  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf15-21

Mapping Theory to Code Entities

The following diagrams illustrate how the abstract mathematical concepts of the MDP and Bellman equations map to the specific structures defined in the cheatsheet and the README.

Diagram 1: MDP Entity Mapping

This diagram bridges the natural language definitions of an MDP to the symbols used throughout the repository's documentation and evaluation files.



Sources:


    

  • README.md5-11
    • 

  • README.md54-58
    • 



Diagram 2: Bellman Equation Data Flow


This diagram shows how the standard components are reorganized into the specific variables ($g, o, q, v$) used in the reformulated dot-product pipeline.




Sources:


    

  • README.md5-11
    • 

  • README.md35-38
    • 

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf15-21
    • 



Limitations of the Standard Form


The repository argues that the standard scalar/summation form and the matrix/operator form represent two extremes:


    

  1. Scalar Form: Precise but computationally opaque due to heavy use of $\sum$.
    2. 

  2. Matrix Form: Elegant but often too abstract for direct implementation or student comprehension.
    3. 



The vectorized dot-product approach serves as a "middle path" README.md26-28 by exposing the "computational skeleton" of the Bellman equation in a form that is immediately translatable to linear algebra operations.


Sources:


    

  • README.md15-32
    •

Motivation and Background



Relevant source files

    

  • README.md
    • 





This page outlines the rationale behind the vector dot-product reformulation of the Bellman Expectation Equation. It explores the limitations of traditional mathematical representations in Reinforcement Learning (RL) and explains how this "middle path" provides a clearer bridge between theoretical MDP foundations and practical computational implementation.


Purpose and Scope


The primary goal of this project is to provide a concrete linear algebra framework for the Bellman Expectation Equation README.md46-48 By decomposing the standard expectation into four distinct vector operations, the repository aims to assist students, practitioners, and researchers in visualizing the "computational skeleton" of RL value functions README.md26-28


Limitations of Standard Forms


The author identifies a gap between the two most common ways the Bellman Equation is presented in literature:


RepresentationSource StyleAdvantagesLimitations
Scalar/SummationSutton & BartoHighly precise and explicit.The underlying structure is often buried in nested sigma notation, making it difficult to parse quickly README.md21-22
Matrix/OperatorTheoretical PapersElegant, concise, and mathematically powerful.Often too abstract for those trying to understand the step-by-step flow of data and rewards README.md24-25


The "Middle Path": Vector Dot Products


This reformulation introduces a sequence of four explicit steps that transform the abstract expectation over probability distributions into a series of dot products README.md26-28 This approach makes the equation immediately readable to anyone with basic linear algebra knowledge README.md28


The Four-Step Pipeline


The reformulation breaks the Bellman Expectation Equation into a data flow consisting of three dot products and one vector addition.


Conceptual Data Flow


The following diagram illustrates how the system moves from raw rewards and discounts to final state values using the entities defined in the project.


Diagram: Bellman Vectorization Pipeline




Sources: README.md5-11 README.md33-38


Notation Systems


To ensure broad applicability, the project provides the reformulation in two distinct mathematical languages:


    

  1. Gibbs Notation: The traditional vector notation using arrows (e.g., $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{q}$) to denote spatial orientation and dot products README.md30
    2. 

  2. Einstein Notation: A summation convention used in physics and high-performance computing that simplifies index-heavy expressions README.md31
    3. 



Origins and Acknowledgements


The motivation for this project stemmed from graduate studies under Professor Ausif Mahmood, who encouraged a deep dive into the derivation of Bellman Equations README.md63-65 The author, Khosro Pourkavoos, developed this succinct formulation to bridge the gap between abstract theory and the "computational skeleton" required for implementation README.md27-28 README.md61


The development process was also supported by AI collaboration, specifically using Claude (Anthropic) and Gemini (Google) as partners in the mathematical quest README.md67


Sources: README.md61-68

Repository Contents and File Guide



Relevant source files

    

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
    • 

  • Claude_Comment.pdf

  • time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf
    • 





This page provides a technical map of the repository, detailing the contents of each document, the relationships between the mathematical formulations, and the evaluation datasets provided by various AI models.


Document Architecture Overview


The repository is structured to move from high-level summaries (README and Cheatsheet) to deep-dive theoretical supplements and finally to empirical AI evaluations. The core of the project is the reformulation of the Bellman Expectation Equation into a four-step vector dot-product pipeline.


Core System Diagram: Information Flow


The following diagram illustrates how the mathematical concepts flow from the primary cheatsheet into implementation strategies and subsequent AI validation.


Information & Data Flow




Sources: README.md1-40 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-22




File Descriptions


1. Primary Documentation


    

  • README.md: Serves as the entry point. It defines the four core equations README.md5-11 and explains the motivation for the "middle path" between scalar summation and abstract matrix operators README.md17-28
    • 

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf: The central technical reference. It provides the full derivation and presentation of the equations in both Gibbs (vector arrow) and Einstein (index) notations README.md29-32
    • 



2. Theoretical Supplement


    

  • time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf: This document addresses the organizational logic of value functions. It contrasts "Time-Indexed" (temporal axis/sequential updates) with "Space-Indexed" (state-space axis/tabular layouts) time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf1-25 This is critical for translating the dot-product math into efficient data structures in code.
    • 



3. AI Evaluation Suite


The repository includes a series of PDF evaluations where the reformulation was stress-tested by Large Language Models. These files contain screenshots and generated text analyzing the mathematical correctness and pedagogical value of the work.


FileModelKey Content
Claude_Evaluation.pdfAnthropic ClaudeVisual capture of the initial model response Claude_Evaluation.pdf1-48
Claude_Comment.pdfAnthropic ClaudeDetailed technical commentary and formatting Claude_Comment.pdf1-20
Gemini_Evaluation.pdfGoogle GeminiDerivation of $o$, $q$, and $v$ vectors Gemini_Evaluation.pdf1-11
Deepseek_Evaluation.pdfDeepSeek-V3Evaluation within MoE/MLA architecture context Deepseek_Evaluation.pdf1-15
Kimi_Evaluation.pdfKimiObservations on vectorized formulation efficiency Kimi_Evaluation.pdf1-15




Technical Mapping: Math to Representation


The following diagram bridges the natural language descriptions of Reinforcement Learning concepts to the specific vector entities defined in the cheatsheet and used throughout the PDFs.


Conceptual to Vector Entity Mapping




Sources: README.md33-38 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-10




Navigation Guide


To effectively navigate this repository:


    

  1. Start with README.md to understand the high-level syntax README.md5-12
    2. 

  2. Consult Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf for the formal mathematical definitions and notation options (Gibbs vs. Einstein) README.md29-32
    3. 

  3. Review time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf before attempting implementation to decide on the data layout (temporal vs. spatial) time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf6-13
    4. 

  4. Examine the Evaluation PDFs (e.g., Claude_Comment.pdf) to see how the equations are interpreted by AI agents, which can highlight potential edge cases or deeper theoretical implications Claude_Comment.pdf10-12
    5. 



Sources: README.md40-43 time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf1-25

The Four-Step Vector Dot-Product Reformulation



Relevant source files

    

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
    • 

  • README.md
    • 





This page provides a deep dive into the core mathematical contribution of this repository: the decomposition of the Bellman Expectation Equation into four discrete vector operations. By moving away from traditional scalar summation notation (Sutton & Barto style) and abstract matrix operators, this reformulation exposes the computational skeleton of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in a format directly compatible with modern linear algebra libraries and GPU-accelerated computing README.md15-28


The Computational Pipeline


The reformulation breaks down the complex recursive relationship of the Bellman equation into a linear pipeline. This separation allows for modular implementation where each component (rewards, transitions, policies) is treated as a distinct vector entity README.md33-38


1. Discounted Return ($g = \vec{\gamma} \cdot \vec{r}$)


The first step represents the return ($g$) as the dot product of a discount vector and a reward vector. This transformation shifts the view of "return" from a temporal sum to a spatial alignment between the agent's horizon and the received rewards README.md35


2. Outcome Vector ($\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma\vec{v}'$)


The "Outcome Vector" (one-step Bellman backup) represents the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the subsequent state. This vector captures the potential value of all possible next states before transition probabilities are applied README.md36


3. State-Action Value ($q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$)


The Action-Value ($q$) is calculated as the expected outcome. By taking the dot product of the transition probability vector ($\vec{p}$) and the outcome vector ($\vec{o}$), we compute the value of taking a specific action in a specific state README.md37


4. State Value ($v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$)


The final step calculates the State-Value ($v$) as the expected $q$-value under the current policy. This is the dot product of the policy distribution vector ($\vec{\pi}$) and the vector of action values ($\vec{q}$) README.md38


Data Flow and Logic


The following diagram illustrates how data flows through these four equations to compute the value of a state.


Bellman Vector Flow




Sources: README.md5-11 README.md33-38


Implementation Mapping


The reformulation bridges the gap between the "Natural Language Space" of RL theory and the "Code Entity Space" of implementation. The variables used in the cheatsheet map directly to data structures typically found in RL environments (like OpenAI Gym/Farama Gymnasium) and solver implementations.


Theory to Code Entity Mapping


Reformulation StepMathematical SymbolCode Identifier (Typical)Data Structure
Discounted Return$g$discounted_returnScalar / Float
Outcome Vector$\vec{o}$outcome_vector1D Array / Tensor
Transition Probability$\vec{p}$P[s][a]1D Probability Vector
Action Value$q$q_table[s][a]Scalar / Float
State Value$v$v_table[s]Scalar / Float
Policy$\vec{\pi}$policy[s]1D Probability Vector


Code Structure Visualization




Sources: README.md5-11 README.md26-32 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-10


Why Separate the Steps?


Standard RL textbooks often combine these steps into a single nested summation:
$$V(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a|s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r|s, a) [r + \gamma V(s')]$$


The four-step reformulation is preferred for technical implementation because:


    

  1. Debugging: Each intermediate vector ($\vec{o}$ and $\vec{q}$) can be inspected independently to verify transition logic versus policy logic README.md21-28
    2. 

  2. Vectorization: Modern hardware (CPUs with AVX, GPUs with CUDA) is optimized for dot products. By expressing the Bellman equation as $\vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$, we leverage high-performance BLAS libraries README.md26-28
    3. 

  3. Clarity: It separates the "Physics" of the environment (the transition vector $\vec{p}$ and reward $\vec{r}$) from the "Intelligence" of the agent (the policy vector $\vec{\pi}$) README.md46-52
    4. 



Sources:


    

  • README.md15-39
    • 

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-23
    •

  • 3. State-Action Value ($q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$)

The Vectorized Bellman Equation



Relevant source files

    

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
    • 

  • README.md
    • 





The Vectorized Bellman Equation represents a mathematical reformulation of the standard Bellman Expectation Equation. By replacing traditional scalar summation (Sigma notation) with explicit vector dot products, this approach bridges the gap between abstract reinforcement learning theory and concrete linear algebra implementation.


The core contribution of this repository is a four-step computational pipeline that decomposes the Bellman backup into distinct, readable vector operations README.md15-28 This formulation is presented in two distinct systems: Gibbs Notation (traditional arrow vectors) and Einstein Notation (index summation convention) README.md29-31


Conceptual Framework


The following diagram illustrates how the mathematical components of the vectorized Bellman equation map to the conceptual entities within a Markov Decision Process (MDP).


MDP to Vector Mapping




Sources: README.md33-38 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf1-10


The Four-Step Pipeline


The reformulation breaks down the complex Bellman Expectation Equation into four sequential dot-product steps. This modularity allows for clearer debugging and a more direct translation to vectorized code (such as NumPy or PyTorch).


    

  1. Discounted Return ($g$): Calculated as the dot product of the discount vector and the reward vector: $g = \vec{\gamma} \cdot \vec{r}$ README.md35
    2. 

  2. Outcome Vector ($\vec{o}$): Represents the one-step Bellman backup, combining immediate reward with discounted future state values: $\vec{o} = \vec{r} + \gamma\vec{v}'$ README.md36
    3. 

  3. State-Action Value ($q$): The expected outcome for a specific action, found by the dot product of transition probabilities and the outcome vector: $q = \vec{p} \cdot \vec{o}$ README.md37
    4. 

  4. State Value ($v$): The expected $Q$-value across all possible actions under the current policy: $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$ README.md38
    5. 



Data Flow Diagram




Sources: README.md5-11 README.md33-38


Detailed Topic Links


This section is a parent page for the following detailed technical guides:


MDP Foundations and the Standard Bellman Equation


Covers the mathematical prerequisites, including Markov Decision Processes, the standard scalar/summation form used by Sutton & Barto, and the definitions of $V(s)$, $Q(s,a)$, and $\pi$.
For details, see MDP Foundations and the Standard Bellman Equation.


The Four-Step Vector Dot-Product Reformulation


A deep dive into the four core equations. This section explains the derivation of each step and why separating the "Outcome Vector" from the "State-Action Value" provides computational clarity.
For details, see The Four-Step Vector Dot-Product Reformulation.


Gibbs Notation vs. Einstein Notation


Compares the two notation systems used in the project's cheatsheet. Gibbs notation uses traditional vector arrows ($\vec{v} \cdot \vec{q}$), while Einstein notation uses index conventions ($v = \pi_a q^a$) favored in physics and advanced tensor calculus.
For details, see Gibbs Notation vs. Einstein Notation.


Time-Indexed vs. Space-Indexed Representations


Based on the supplementary documentation, this section explores how value functions are organized. It contrasts temporal axis updates (sequential) with spatial axis layouts (tabular/state-space), discussing the trade-offs for RL implementation.
For details, see Time-Indexed vs. Space-Indexed Representations.

Time-Indexed vs. Space-Indexed Representations



Relevant source files

    

  • Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf
    • 

  • time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf
    • 





This page explores the dual perspectives of organizing value functions in Reinforcement Learning: Time-Indexed (sequential, trajectory-based) and Space-Indexed (tabular, state-space-based). It details how the vectorized Bellman reformulation bridges these two representations to facilitate efficient computation and implementation.


Overview and Scope


The supplementary document time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf provides a theoretical and practical framework for understanding how Reinforcement Learning (RL) data structures are laid out in memory and processed by algorithms time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf1-25 While standard textbooks often treat the Bellman equation as a scalar update for a single state, implementation requires choosing between organizing data by time steps ($t, t+1, \dots$) or by state-action indices ($s, a$).


Key Definitions


    

  • Time-Indexed: Data is organized according to the sequence of experience. The primary axis is the time step $t$. This is the natural representation for online learning and trajectory sampling.
    • 

  • Space-Indexed: Data is organized according to the topology of the state-action space. The primary axis is the state index $s$ or action index $a$. This is the natural representation for dynamic programming and tabular value iteration.
    • 



Sources: time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf3-13 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-10




The Vectorized Bridge


The core contribution of this repository—the four-step dot-product reformulation—acts as the mathematical bridge between these two representations. By treating outcomes and values as vectors, the transition from a temporal sequence to a spatial table becomes a matter of linear algebra operations.


Data Flow: From Trajectory to Table


The following diagram illustrates how the system transitions from time-indexed experience (trajectories) to space-indexed value functions using the dot-product framework.


Diagram: Temporal to Spatial Data Flow




Sources: time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf10-20 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-8




Implementation Trade-offs


Choosing between time-indexed and space-indexed representations impacts memory locality, cache performance, and the choice of optimization algorithms.


FeatureTime-Indexed (Sequential)Space-Indexed (Tabular)
Primary AxisTime $t$State $s$, Action $a$
Data StructureExperience Replay Buffer / ArraysMatrices / Tensors
Key OperationSampling / Sequential UpdatesDot Products / Matrix Multiplications
Memory AccessLinear / SequentialRandom / Indexed
Algorithm FitSARSA, Q-Learning (Online)Value Iteration, Policy Iteration


Relationship to Code Entities


The mathematical entities defined in the cheatsheet Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-15 map directly to implementation structures in RL libraries.


Diagram: Math to Code Mapping




Sources: Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-12 time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf15-23




Technical Detail: Indexing Transformations


The document explains that the "State Value" $v$ is inherently space-indexed (indexed by state), while the "Return" $g$ is inherently time-indexed (indexed by time step) time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf8-12


1. The Outcome Vector ($o$)


The outcome vector $o$ serves as the transformation point. In a time-indexed view, $o_t$ is the one-step lookahead for a specific moment. In a space-indexed view, $\vec{o}$ represents the potential outcomes for all possible next states from a given $(s, a)$ pair Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-7


2. Tabular vs. Function Approximation


    

  • Tabular (Space-Indexed): The value function is a literal vector where the index corresponds to the state ID. The dot product $v = \vec{\pi} \cdot \vec{q}$ is a direct lookup and multiply-accumulate operation time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf20-22
    • 

  • Function Approximation: The space-indexed representation is implicit. The "vector" is the output of a neural network for all possible inputs. However, the dot-product formulation remains valid by treating the network's output layer as the vector components time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf23-25
    • 



Sources: time-indexed-versus-space-indexed.pdf8-25 Bellman_Equation__Khosro_Pourkavoos__cheatsheet.pdf6-10
